title: OPT
description: Meta's Open Pre-trained Transformer line, published to give researchers a GPT-style model family with weights and training details they could inspect.
openingSummary: OPT matters because many readers need a named example of a GPT-style language model that was released with an unusually explicit openness goal. The important idea is not that OPT changed the architecture class. It is that OPT made a large decoder-only model family easier to study directly.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: OPT is Meta's Open Pre-trained Transformer model line. It is a family of decoder-only language models released to make large GPT-style systems more inspectable to researchers.

inputsAndOutputs.title: Inputs And Outputs
inputsAndOutputs.body: OPT takes tokenized text and predicts the next token in sequence. That makes the model easy to place alongside GPT-like systems and other autoregressive language-model families.

architecture.title: Architecture
architecture.body: OPT follows the standard Transformer decoder-only pattern rather than introducing a new model class. The page is useful because it separates the familiar architecture from the unusual release goal of sharing a large-scale open research reference.

importantModules.title: Important Modules
importantModules.body: Attention is the main module to understand because OPT is a straightforward causal language model. The most useful adjacent pages are therefore Transformer architecture, decoder-only models, and autoregressive generation.

training.title: Training
training.body: Public OPT documentation emphasizes the effort to release large pretrained models, weights, and training notes more openly than many peers. Structured training-regime and dataset relationships can still be incomplete in this atlas, so the page keeps those sections visible with explicit unavailable states.

practicalNotes.title: Practical Notes
practicalNotes.body: OPT remains a useful historical page because it gave people a public large-model reference for studying GPT-style systems. The name often appears when readers are tracing the open-model lineage before the later wave of Llama, Mistral, and similar releases.
